inline_comments.message:
the code should perform a "GET" on the topic before it commences execution - that will ensure that the topic has been created before anything tries to post to it

the other robot scripts use make_topic.sh to create the topic and wait_topic.sh to wait for a message containing various text.  Perhaps those should be used here, too, for consistency

I was trying to use wait_topic.sh. But realized that it uses "https://${SIM_IP}:3905/events/${topic}/script/1?limit=1". limit is 1, I think drools is using it extensively.
It seems using that script makes it more tedious instead of making it simpler in this case, because I will have to call it 3 times for the expected  message in this case(I am expecting 3 different events on DMaaP). If I update the limit from 1 to 3, it may need more work on drools csit I guess.
Calling the script 3 times seems less efficient to me, but I am okay to do it to keep it consisent. Comments?

Will work on the other comments.

that makes sense, though it may make the test fragile, as the simulator will only return one message, if there's nothing yet on the queue when the GET is performed.  Seems like invoking wait_topic three times might actually be a bit clearer, as it would make it obvious that it's checking three different messages.  OTOH, is it necessary to check for all three, or is it sufficient to just check the last one?

note: still need to perform a make_topic at the very beginning, to ensure the topic has been created

Sure, my question was about the usage of wait_topic script only.
Just checking the last one is enough to check if the policy execution was successful. But, at the final stage(i.e., after CDS action) only 1 output is there, which is the final log message.
Only in the intermediate steps, multiple outputs are made - for e.g., CDSRequest is made, along with the Log message saying "Received response from AAI ... Going to make the update-config request to CDS."
So, to test multiple outputs as well the complete execution, all log messages have to be checked.

Done. Removed the Sleep 2 sec I added earlier. Seems efficient enough on execution.

if this were added to the wait_topic command line, then wait_topic would automatically skip over any messages that contain something else and would exit with 0 when it finds a match.  I don't think it's necessary to check the exit code (though I could be wrong).  Then I believe this entire "function" would simply become:

  wait_topic.sh ... ${expectedMsg}
nvm - you DO need to check the result code.  So my only comment here is to remove line 116 and add the expectedMsg to the wait_topic command line
is the export needed, didn't see it in other files?

I actually tried that approach, but felt this is more ideal in this case.
Because, 1) When a different message is received, the failure message will clearly say what didn't match with what.
2) DMaaP simulator anyway returns the message in  order (that is what I saw during all the csit executions), hence I can read 1 by 1 and check for the expected message. If there is a mismatch, then it will be clearly printed.

Just calling wait_topic.sh with the expected message will work(just checking exitcode will be enough then), but during failures, what message came in the topic will not be visible.

I think drools also uses similar approach - listen in the topic with something unique in the event, then check if the expected things are there in the event.
Yes, without this, SIM_IP will not be avialble in the wait_topic.sh script.
could delete DMAAP_IP, then, as it's redundant

Yes, but I can't just use ${SIM_IP} in the robot scripts, for that we should pass it as a variable. Otherwise, we have to do something like: ${SIM_IP}=    Get Environment Variable  SIM_IP in the robot file.
nvm - there is no "DMAAP_IP" variable to be deleted from this script.  Could change the robot script to use SIM_IP to reduce confusion, but it's OK as is, too.
It's really good to see a more real world example policy being tested in CSIT every time. Thanks Ajith!
